schools.summary: Joseph's respectful report — though favourite and prime minister, and though Pharaoh had invited the family, Joseph would not settle them till he gave the king notice and obtained leave (Henry, the great man not presuming on his power); 'they are in the land of Goshen' (announcing their arrival and halt in the region he wished for them); much as he desired Goshen, the grant lay with Pharaoh, so he mentions only that they stopped there (Ellicott); indirectly intimating his wish with a modesty free from cunning (Calvin).

themes: my father and my brethren are come

schools.summary: The five brothers presented — the curious Hebrew 'from the extremity of his brethren he took five men,' understood several ways (Clarke), some taking it of the meanest or least imposing (lest Pharaoh enlist the most capable as soldiers, per Jewish tradition); Joseph chose five as representatives for the royal audience; the recurring number five in the narrative (Gen 43:34; 45:22) perhaps of special significance among the Egyptians as seven among the Hebrews (Ellicott); a chosen few suiting the formality of a royal audience.

The question and confession of calling — Pharaoh's 'What is your occupation?' (the question Joseph foretold; all who have a place should have an employment, Wesley); 'Thy servants are shepherds, both we, and also our fathers' (owning their trade frankly, adding 'our fathers' by Joseph's instruction since callings were hereditary in Egypt, so no change of life was expected, disposing Pharaoh to grant separate pasture, Ellicott); the confession humiliating, for the Egyptians held shepherding base and infamous (Calvin); the providence — Egyptian contempt keeping the Hebrews apart in Goshen, preserving the holy seed.

The request to sojourn in Goshen — 'to sojourn in the land are we come' (only a temporary stay, not permanent possession; keeping in mind that Canaan, not Egypt, was promised to them, Gill); the reason 'no pasture for their flocks; the famine is sore in Canaan' (the dearth leaving their cattle without grazing in scorched Canaan, while Goshen, watered by the Nile, still afforded pasture); 'let thy servants dwell in Goshen' (the region Joseph wished, a border pasture-land fit for shepherds, set apart from the Egyptians); not to settle for ever but while the famine prevailed (Wesley).

themes: to sojourn in the land are we come
let thy servants dwell in Goshen

schools.summary: Pharaoh's gracious turning to Joseph — Joseph stood present throughout the audience, hearing all (Gill); the king speaks now to Joseph, 'thy father and thy brethren are come unto thee,' committing the settlement to his trusted minister; the providence (Calvin) — it is to be ascribed to God's special favour that Pharaoh took no offence at their desire to dwell apart in Goshen, for kings bear nothing more indignantly than to have their favours restricted, yet he freely grants their wish; God inclining the king's heart to allow the separateness the Hebrews needed.

themes: thy father and thy brethren are come

schools.summary: Pharaoh's generous grant — 'the land of Egypt is before thee' (the whole land at Joseph's disposal); 'in the best of the land... in Goshen' (assigning the region they desired, Goshen thereby the choicest part, Clarke); 'men of activity... make them rulers over my cattle' (anshey chayil, robust able men entrusted with the royal herds); all given at the king's command, not Joseph's private disposal — the higher his power, the more scrupulous not to be liberal with the king's goods without warrant (Calvin); the king's liberality exceeding the request, the fruit of his regard for Joseph and God's favour.

themes: the land of Egypt is before thee

schools.summary: The patriarch's blessing of the king — the presentation of Jacob a more solemn matter than the brethren's (Ellicott), Pharaoh receiving the venerable father; 'Jacob blessed Pharaoh' (on entering, pronouncing a blessing); in part a courteous salutation, 'peace be unto thee' (Clarke), but more an act of piety — with the gravity of age, the faith of a believer, and the authority of a patriarch and prophet, beseeching God to bless Pharaoh (Henry); the dignity — a poor aged sojourning shepherd blessing the mighty king, the lesser in estate conferring the greater spiritual gift, as heir of the promises.

The king's question about Jacob's age — 'How old art thou?' (Heb. 'how many are the days of the years of thy life?', a phrasing Jacob takes up in his answer, Gill); the wonder behind it — Pharaoh perhaps never saw so aged a man, for in Egypt life was shorter and such age rare (Lightfoot via Gill); Jacob's countenance, worn by labour and sorrow, bespeaking an extraordinary span; the question naturally put to the old, for it is native to admire and reverence age (Wesley); even mighty Pharaoh moved to wonder before the aged saint.

themes: the days of the years of thy life

schools.summary: Jacob's confession of a pilgrim life — 'my pilgrimage' (megurai, sojournings, wandering; he lived a migratory life in Canaan, Mesopotamia, Egypt, scarcely at rest, Clarke; a stranger settled in a land not his own, 1 Pet 2:11, Ellicott); the deeper sense — Jacob a stranger and pilgrim in the world itself, a traveller toward a heavenly country, the believer not at home on earth (Wesley, Gill); 'an hundred and thirty years... few and evil,' short and full of trouble, not reaching his fathers' span; not murmuring but owning the brevity and hardship, his hope resting beyond (Calvin).

The parting blessing — as on entering (v7), so departing, 'he blessed Pharaoh, and went out' (the audience framed in benediction); wishing the king all happiness and acknowledging the honour shown his house (Gill); more than courtesy, an act of piety — Jacob praying for Pharaoh as one bearing the authority of a prophet and patriarch, a blessing not to be despised even by a potent prince (Wesley); the heir of the covenant, though a poor sojourner, in a place to bless the king of Egypt, the lesser conferring the greater gift.

The settlement in the best of the land — 'Joseph placed his father and brethren, and gave them a possession' (a fixed dwelling and holding — houses, land to till, pasture for flocks and herds, Gill); 'in the best of the land, in the land of Rameses' (the choicest district of Goshen, named for one of its cities, the fertile border-land suited to shepherds); 'as Pharaoh had commanded' (Joseph acting strictly by the king's order, not his own authority); the providence — the family established in a rich, separate region to grow apart from the Egyptians, secure through the famine.

themes: Joseph placed his father and brethren
a possession in the best of the land

schools.summary: Joseph's provision for his kin — 'Joseph nourished his father, and brethren, and all his father's household, with bread' (though Goshen afforded pasture, it could not feed them with corn while the famine continued, so he supplied bread for all, Gill); 'according to their families' (the Hebrew taph, according to the number of children and dependants in each household, the provision apportioned to need, so even the brother with one child and the brother with many were each supplied, none starved, Ellicott); the idiom 'to the mouth of the little one,' feeding them tenderly (Calvin); the brother once sold now the sustainer.

themes: Joseph nourished his father and brethren

schools.summary: The severity of the famine — 'there was no bread in all the land' (private stores exhausted, bread only from Pharaoh's granaries Joseph had filled); 'the famine was very sore, so that Egypt and Canaan fainted' (tilah, to languish, be utterly exhausted, both countries drained by hunger); a memorable judgment of God that the most fertile regions, wont to supply distant nations, were reduced nearly to be consumed (Calvin); the chapter recording God's saving of Egypt from ruin, for he is King of nations as well as of saints (Henry).

themes: no bread in all the land

schools.summary: The gathering of the money — 'Joseph gathered up all the money... for the corn which they bought' (not by exaction but selling corn from the royal stores to starving Egypt and Canaan, till all the money in both lands was paid in, Clarke); 'brought the money into Pharaoh's house' (the whole proceeds to the king's treasury); the fidelity Moses commends (Calvin) — Joseph, with sole charge of the corn and handling vast sums, kept nothing back for himself, incorruptibly honest where few could resist enriching themselves; the perishing fed and the crown enriched together, conducted with order and justice.

themes: for the corn which they bought

schools.summary: The failing of the money and the people's cry — 'when money failed in Egypt and Canaan' (the silver of both lands exhausted after years of buying corn, spent through the earlier famine years, Gill); not that every coin was in the treasury — some nobles still had means — but nearly all gone, the common people destitute (Calvin); 'all the Egyptians came... Give us bread' (the starving thronging to him who alone has corn); 'why should we die in thy presence? for the money faileth' (perishing before his eyes with nothing to pay, casting themselves on his mercy); the famine driving them from one extremity to the next.

themes: why should we die in thy presence?

schools.summary: The exchange of cattle for bread — 'Give your cattle... if money fail' (the silver gone, Joseph accepting livestock as the price of grain); the wisest measure for preserving both people and cattle, since the people had no grain to feed their beasts, which must else have starved — surrendering them, the owners were fed and the cattle kept alive in Pharaoh's keeping (Clarke); a merciful arrangement relieving the owners of a burden, a portion saved (Ellicott); not extortion but a prudent stewardship dispensing the stores justly (Calvin).

themes: I will give you for your cattle
the exchange relieves man and beast

schools.summary: The year sustained by cattle — 'they brought their cattle unto Joseph' (the more readily since there was scarce any grass left to feed them, to surrender them no great loss but a relief, Gill); the full list exchanged — 'horses... flocks... herds... asses'; the mention of horses a significant fact for dating the period, the horse a late arrival in Egypt not known in Abraham's time, pointing to the dynasty Joseph served (Ellicott); 'he fed them with bread for all their cattle for that year' (the surrender buying a whole further year of sustenance, the nation carried through a second stage of want).

themes: bread in exchange for horses and flocks

schools.summary: The last extremity — 'when that year was ended, they came the second year' (not the second year of the famine but the year after surrendering the cattle, the seventh and last of the dearth, when the land must be sown for a future crop, Clarke/Ellicott); the confession 'our money is spent; my lord hath our cattle; there is not ought left but our bodies and our lands' (stripped by stages — silver, livestock, now only persons and fields remain); knowing from Joseph's prophecy the end was near, they sought seed as well as food, that the land might yield again (Calvin); the whole nation at its last possession.

themes: nothing left but our bodies and lands

schools.summary: The people's offer of themselves — the desperate proposal made by the people, not imposed: 'buy us and our land for bread, and we and our land will be servants unto Pharaoh' (reduced to extremity, freely offering to become bondservants and give up their fields for bread to live); 'give us seed, that we may live... that the land be not desolate' (asking seed-corn for sowing, looking to a future crop near the famine's end); without bread they must die and without seed their land too (Gill); in such straits men have even sold children and families to escape starvation (Clarke); prizing life above freedom.

themes: buy us and our land for bread

schools.summary: The purchase of the land — 'Joseph bought all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh... so the land became Pharaoh's' (the Egyptians selling their fields for bread, the king made sole owner); bought for Pharaoh, not for himself nor entailed on his family, his integrity preserved (Gill); the gain wholly to the crown.

debates.question: Was Joseph's land policy oppressive, reducing a free people to slavery?

sides.position: no — though it greatly increased royal power, it broke the yoke of the petty lords and was a just, necessary administration in a public emergency, the people themselves consenting and offering it
the accusation that Joseph reduced a free people to slavery and concentrated all power in the king
holders: some critics (noted and rejected by the expositors)

themes: Joseph bought all the land of Egypt

schools.summary: The removal of the people — 'he removed them to cities from one end of the borders of Egypt to the other' (the whole population shifted near the cities); read mercifully — Joseph moved not by political design but humanity and prudence, the corn being stored in the cities so the people could be surely fed near it (Clarke, Ellicott); a second purpose — displaying Pharaoh's sovereign right over the purchased land and helping them forget their old titles (Wesley); the removal severe yet better that they remain free cultivators than serfs bound to the old soil (Calvin); generally defended as wise and humane.

themes: from one end of Egypt to the other

schools.summary: The exemption of the priests — 'only the land of the priests bought he not' (their estate not taken amid the universal sale); the reason the text gives — 'the priests had a portion assigned them of Pharaoh, and did eat their portion... wherefore they sold not their lands'; not from any affection or superstitious reverence Joseph bore the heathen priests, which could never be thought of so good a man, but because they had no need to sell, the king allowing them a fixed maintenance (Gill, Calvin); the religion of Egypt established and state-supported, the priests long receiving a daily allowance (Clarke, Ellicott, Herodotus).

themes: the land of the priests bought he not

schools.summary: The giving of seed — 'I have bought you this day and your land for Pharaoh' (stating the new state, people and land now the crown's); 'lo, here is seed for you, and ye shall sow the land' (the famine closing, Joseph furnishing the seed-corn they asked, that they plant and reap again; the timing showing the arrangement completed near the seventh year's end when sowing could resume, Ellicott); Joseph's singular humanity reinstating the destitute, exiles on their own soil, setting them to work again as Pharaoh's tenants (Calvin); Egypt before Joseph a limited monarchy, now the people holding their land of Pharaoh but tilling it still.

themes: I have bought you and your land

schools.summary: The moderate rent — 'ye shall give the fifth part unto Pharaoh, and four parts shall be your own' (of each future harvest, a fifth to the crown as rent, four fifths to the people); the four parts 'for seed... for your food... for your households... for your little ones' (enough for sowing, sustenance, and families, an ample tenant's portion); the rent light and even generous — a fifth to the sovereign who bought the land and saved their lives, the cultivators keeping four fifths; the fifth a recognized, not oppressive tax, as in India (Clarke); the moderation answering the charge of cruelty.

themes: four parts shall be your own
for seed and food and little ones

schools.summary: The people's gratitude — 'Thou hast saved our lives' (owning that Joseph by his foresight and administration preserved them from death by famine, thankfully confessing the favour, Gill); 'let us find grace... and we will be Pharaoh's servants' (accepting their new condition gladly, content as the king's tenants, asking only continued favour); the people more than satisfied — if made dependent on Pharaoh, they were freed from the yoke of the petty lords, their lot bettered (Ellicott); their grateful response the strongest answer to the charge that Joseph reduced them cruelly to slavery — the saved blessing their deliverer.

The lasting statute — 'Joseph made it a law over the land of Egypt unto this day, that Pharaoh should have the fifth part' (the emergency measure become a fixed law, enacted with the consent of Pharaoh and people, a fundamental law of the constitution still in force in Moses's day and after, Gill); the moderation — the people held their land of the king and rendered a fifth as yearly tax, the crown demanding no more, the burden light and the holdings secure (Clarke); the standing exception 'except the land of the priests' (free of tax and sale); the permanence showing the wisdom and acceptability of the policy.

themes: Pharaoh should have the fifth part
except the land of the priests

schools.summary: The prospering of Israel — 'Israel dwelt in Goshen; and they had possessions therein' (fixed in the granted region, holding lands and houses), yet dwelling not as outright owners in perpetuity but as sojourners granted a dwelling-place, their true inheritance still Canaan (Calvin); 'grew, and multiplied exceedingly' (the household of seventy beginning to multiply into the promised nation, the famine become the occasion of Israel's growth); the contrast — while the Egyptians were impoverished in their own land, the sojourning Israelites were replenished and prospered (Henry); the removal to Egypt the means of preservation and increase.

Jacob's years in Egypt — 'Jacob lived in Egypt seventeen years' (granted, beyond his expectation of soon dying, to see his family established and Joseph's prosperity); the symmetry — as Jacob nourished and reared Joseph the first seventeen years before losing him, so Joseph nourished his father seventeen years in his old age, Providence ordering a fitting return (Wesley); the same number of years with Joseph in Egypt as Joseph had with him before (Gill); 'the whole age... an hundred forty and seven,' a long pilgrimage yet short of his fathers'; no small trial to live so long an exile, yet faith holding its hope in Canaan (Calvin).

themes: Jacob lived in Egypt seventeen years
an hundred forty and seven years

schools.summary: Jacob's charge concerning his burial — 'the time drew nigh that Israel must die' (he who prevailed with the angel must yet yield to death, his candle gradually burning down, Wesley); 'put thy hand under my thigh' (the ancient form of solemn oath, as Abraham's servant, Gen 24:2, Clarke); 'deal kindly and truly... bury me not in Egypt' (asking the last kindness, his body not left in the land of sojourning); the faith and magnanimity (Calvin) — no wealth of Egypt where his son ruled could allure him to rest there, his heart sighing for Canaan; the charge an act of faith that the promised land, not Egypt, is his seed's true inheritance.

themes: the time drew nigh that Israel must die
put thy hand under my thigh

schools.summary: The request to rest with his fathers — 'I will lie with my fathers... carry me out of Egypt, and bury me in their buryingplace' (the cave of Machpelah near Hebron, where Abraham, Sarah, and Isaac lay); a twofold purpose (Gill) — to express Jacob's faith in the promised land, owning Canaan as his seed's inheritance, and to draw his posterity's hearts from Egypt to Canaan lest they forget the promise; Canaan and the patriarchs' grave a consecrated place under God's blessing (Clarke); 'I will sleep' — the word 'sleep' for death referring to the body, not the soul which lives (Calvin); Joseph's ready 'I will do as thou hast said.'

themes: I will lie with my fathers

schools.summary: The oath and Jacob's worship — 'Swear unto me. And he sware unto him' (Jacob binding the promise with an oath, not from distrust of faithful Joseph but to show how intensely his mind was set, making the obligation sure that nothing prevent burial in Canaan, Gill); 'Israel bowed himself upon the bed's head' (the aged patriarch bowing in reverence, content his wish was secured); the textual variation — the Hebrew without vowels reads 'bed' or 'staff,' the LXX and Heb 11:21 'leaning upon the top of his staff,' both agreeing in the act of worship; Jacob exerting his failing body to give thanks and worship God who kept his covenant; his last act one of faith and adoration.

debates.question: Did Israel bow 'upon the bed's head' or 'upon the top of his staff'?

sides.position: a textual variation — the unpointed Hebrew may read 'bed' or 'staff'; the LXX and Hebrews 11:21 read 'leaning on the top of his staff,' both agreeing in the act of worship

themes: Israel bowed upon the bed's head
